title: Object Units or Pixels? A Pre-Registered Benchmark for Online Open-Vocabulary Segmentation Adaptation
description: This project is a benchmark-style empirical paper, not a new OVSS architecture. It isolates one question under a pre-registered online adaptation protocol: with the same backbone, optimizer, LoRA scope, and update budget, are CLIP-verified object units a better pseudo-supervision substrate than pixels, superpixels, or ungated SAM masks?
motivation: Recent 2025 papers already establish that open-vocabulary semantic segmentation can be adapted at test time, and earlier OVSS papers already establish that mask-level CLIP reasoning is useful for inference. What is still missing is a clean, source-held-out comparison that separates substrate choice from objective design, avoids target-stream calibration leakage, and uses a broader standard OVSS-TTA benchmark than a small pilot stream.
proposed_approach: Use one public OVSS backbone, preferably CAT-Seg with CLIP ViT-B/16, and one shared online adaptation recipe: rank-4 LoRA on the last decoder or fusion block, one gradient step per image, and the same pseudo-label plus consistency objective for every adaptive method. Compare four matched-budget substrates, Top-B pixels, SLIC superpixels, raw MobileSAM masks, and CLIP-verified MobileSAM object units, against frozen OVSS and the published MLMP comparator on a 320-image two-order benchmark. Calibrate the acceptance thresholds and per-image support budget once on a disjoint held-out source split, then freeze them for all evaluation runs.

related_work.title: Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models for Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation
related_work.authors: Mehrdad Noori, David Osowiechi, Gustavo Adolfo Vargas Hakim, Ali Bahri, Moslem Yazdanpanah, Sahar Dastani, Farzad Beizaee, Ismail Ben Ayed, Christian Desrosiers
related_work.difference: MLMP is the required direct OVSS-TTA comparator and provides the strongest published benchmark context, but it does not isolate the effect of pseudo-supervision substrate under a shared adaptation recipe.

related_work.title: Test-Time Optimization for Domain Adaptive Open Vocabulary Segmentation
related_work.authors: Ulindu De Silva, Didula Samaraweera, Sasini Wanigathunga, Kavindu Kariyawasam, Kanchana Ranasinghe, Muzammal Naseer, Ranga Rodrigo
related_work.difference: Seg-TTO confirms OVSS test-time adaptation already exists, which is why this proposal avoids any first-of-its-kind framing and instead focuses on controlled substrate comparison.

related_work.title: Semantic Library Adaptation: LoRA Retrieval and Fusion for Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation
related_work.authors: Reza Qorbani, Gianluca Villani, Theodoros Panagiotakopoulos, Marc Botet Colomer, Linus Harenstam-Nielsen, Mattia Segu, Pier Luigi Dovesi, Jussi Karlgren, Daniel Cremers, Matteo Poggi
related_work.difference: SemLA studies stronger source-free OVSS adaptation pipelines, but not the online substrate question under a single fixed objective.

related_work.title: Lost in Translation? Vocabulary Alignment for Source-Free Adaptation in Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation
related_work.authors: Silvio Mazzucco, Carl Persson, Mattia Segu, Pier Luigi Dovesi, Federico Tombari, Luc Van Gool, Matteo Poggi
related_work.difference: VocAlign addresses source-free OVSS adaptation at the vocabulary level, not a pre-registered comparison of support units for online adaptation.

related_work.title: Side Adapter Network for Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation
related_work.authors: Mengde Xu, Zheng Zhang, Fangyun Wei, Han Hu, Xiang Bai
related_work.difference: SAN already establishes mask-centric OVSS inference, so this proposal does not claim novelty from using object-like regions themselves.

related_work.title: Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation with Mask-adapted CLIP
related_work.authors: Feng Liang, Bichen Wu, Xiaoliang Dai, Kunpeng Li, Yinan Zhao, Hang Zhang, Peizhao Zhang, Peter Vajda, Diana Marculescu
related_work.difference: Mask-adapted CLIP already demonstrates CLIP reasoning on masked regions, which is why this paper treats CLIP verification as a controlled benchmark factor rather than a new method claim.

related_work.title: Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation with Image Embedding Balancing
related_work.authors: Xiangheng Shan, Dongyue Wu, Guilin Zhu, Yuanjie Shao, Nong Sang, Changxin Gao
related_work.difference: EBSeg further shows object-aware design matters for OVSS inference, but does not study online adaptation under stream drift.

related_work.title: Effective SAM Combination for Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation
related_work.authors: Minhyeok Lee, Suhwan Cho, Jungho Lee, Sunghun Yang, Heeseung Choi, Ig-Jae Kim, Sangyoun Lee
related_work.difference: ESC-Net shows SAM-assisted OVSS is already crowded, strengthening the case for a benchmark paper rather than another SAM-plus-OVSS method.

related_work.title: Distilling Spectral Graph for Object-Context Aware Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation
related_work.authors: Chanyoung Kim, Dayun Ju, Woojung Han, Ming-Hsuan Yang, Seong Jae Hwang
related_work.difference: This paper reinforces that object-level grouping matters for OVSS, but not whether it is the right substrate for online adaptation.

related_work.title: SAM-MI: A Mask-Injected Framework for Enhancing Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation with SAM
related_work.authors: Lin Chen, Yingjian Zhu, Qi Yang, Xin Niu, Kun Ding, Shiming Xiang
related_work.difference: SAM-MI is another OVSS inference-time SAM integration paper, which further narrows the novelty space for any new mask-enhanced method framing.

related_work.title: Instance-Aware Test-Time Segmentation for Continual Domain Shifts
related_work.authors: Seunghwan Lee, Inyoung Jung, Hojoon Lee, Eunil Park, Sungeun Hong
related_work.difference: This is the closest continual-segmentation reference, but it is not open-vocabulary and does not compare pixels, superpixels, and verified object units under a shared protocol.

related_work.title: TransAdapt: A Transformative Framework for Online Test Time Adaptive Semantic Segmentation
related_work.authors: Debasmit Das, Shubhankar Borse, Hyojin Park, Kambiz Azarian, Hong Cai, Risheek Garrepalli, Fatih Porikli
related_work.difference: TransAdapt motivates order-aware online segmentation evaluation, but it does not address the open-vocabulary substrate selection problem.

hypothesis: Under a source-held-out, pre-registered online OVSS protocol, CLIP-verified MobileSAM object units will provide a more reliable adaptation substrate than matched-budget pixels, superpixels, or ungated SAM masks, yielding higher mean-over-orders mIoU and better transition stability.
success_criteria: On the 320-image two-order benchmark, CLIP-verified object-unit adaptation achieves the best or tied-best mean-over-orders mIoU among the shared-recipe adaptive substrates.
CLIP-verified object-unit adaptation outperforms the matched-budget Top-B pixel baseline under the same LoRA scope, optimizer, and one-step schedule.
CLIP-verified object-unit adaptation outperforms raw SAM-mask adaptation, showing that verification rather than mask presence alone matters.
CLIP-verified object-unit adaptation matches or exceeds SLIC superpixel adaptation, showing gains are not from generic region averaging alone.
The method remains competitive with MLMP on the same benchmark slice while using a much simpler fixed objective, and its gains are not limited to a single threshold setting.
